name: mc_knn_standardization_vol_feature_load_bearing
description: AC-1 property fixture. Two histories that are IDENTICAL except for the volatility of the second 30-day block, which controls today's realized volatility (today_vol is computed from the history tail). H_low has a low-volatility tail; H_high has a high-volatility tail. In BOTH histories the SPY returns of every day cluster around the same value (+0.006 decimal) so the RETURN feature alone cannot distinguish the two blocks. The first block's holding gains (AAA daily_ret +0.020); the second block's holding loses (AAA daily_ret -0.020). Under a standardized kNN the volatility feature must be load-bearing: H_low's low today_vol selects the first (calm) block and H_high's high today_vol selects the second (crash) block, so the two histories MUST produce a different prob_beating. Under the unscaled pre-fix kNN the vol feature is dominated and both histories collapse to the same regime selection (same prob_beating) -- the bug. This is the property assertion: 'neither feature dominates'; here it is checked as 'the vol feature is not silently dominated -- changing only the vol feature changes the selected regime.'
provenance: Schema-derived synthetic history. The two histories differ only in the crash-block SPY dispersion; the holding returns and the calm block are identical.
property: prob_beating(H_low) != prob_beating(H_high). The only difference between the two histories is the second block's volatility; if the standardized vol feature is load-bearing the differing today_vol selects different regimes and the holding's opposite-sign returns make prob_beating differ. A unit-dominated (unscaled) feature vector ignores the vol difference and yields equal prob_beating.
derivation: Verified: pre-fix both histories return prob_beating 100.0 (vol feature dominated -- the calm block wins both because its returns hug today's return). Post-fix the two must diverge. No producer float is pinned; the assertion is the inequality.
